graded_decisions.from_session: unknown (predates 30-day order history; no decision log entry exists)
graded_decisions.news_since: Stock fell ~17-20% YTD into late June on worries that Microsoft's ~$190B FY26 AI capex (driven by soaring memory costs) was outpacing returns, then rallied +10.6% in the review window (close $352.42 on 6/25 to $389.79 on 7/2) as institutional money rotated out of AI chip/hardware names into AI software leaders (The Motley Fool, 'Why Microsoft Stock Surged Today,' 7/1/2026; Invezz, 7/1/2026).
graded_decisions.comment: No original entry reasoning exists in the decision log, so the thesis can't be checked against what was actually claimed. Azure (+40%) and Cloud (+29%) growth support the stock being oversold, but this week's bounce looks like a sector-rotation trade (chips->software) ahead of the late-July earnings print, not confirmed new fundamentals. Still down 8.2% since entry -- treat as unresolved until earnings, not validated.

graded_decisions.from_session: unknown (predates 30-day order history; no decision log entry exists)
graded_decisions.news_since: Jensen Huang conceded Nvidia has 'largely conceded' China's advanced AI chip market to Huawei (near-zero China data-center revenue), and B200 GPU lease pricing fell from $6.11/hr to $4.22/hr between 5/30 and 6/21, signaling margin pressure from rising competition -- this despite a record ~$82B revenue quarter (TradingKey, 6/25/2026; Yahoo Finance, June 2026).
graded_decisions.comment: This is a structural headwind, not sentiment noise: lost China market share plus falling GPU lease rates both compress the long-term margin/growth story. Flat this week (-0.7%) looks like stabilization, not reversal. Largest single-name position (15 shares, $2,922 market value) carrying the account's biggest unrealized loss -- needs an explicit invalidation level, not a 'hold and hope for earnings' default.

graded_decisions.news_since: Nasdaq-100 underperformed the S&P 500 this week as investors rotated out of the 80%+ H1 semiconductor/AI rally (Micron, Sandisk, Applied Materials, Intel, Marvell all -9-10% that week) into broader cyclicals, pushing the Dow to records -- described as a healthy 'Great Rotation' (TheStreet, 7/1/2026) -- compounded by a weak June jobs report (+57K vs. consensus, CNBC, 7/2/2026) that favored defensive/broad-market positioning over high-beta tech.
graded_decisions.comment: Down since the 6/16 entry and lagging SPY by ~2pp this week specifically because of the tech-to-cyclicals rotation. Research suggests this reads as short-term profit-taking after an extraordinary H1 semis rally, not a durable leadership change -- Micron is still +260% YTD even after the pullback. Worth monitoring for a second consecutive down week before treating it as more than rotation.

graded_decisions.from_session: unknown (predates 30-day order history; no decision log entry exists)
graded_decisions.news_since: S&P 500/Dow hit record highs this week as capital rotated out of overheated AI/semiconductor names into broader, more cyclical exposure, and a weak June jobs report (+57K vs. consensus) reinforced a shift toward defensive/broad-market positioning (TheStreet, 7/1/2026; CNBC, 7/2/2026).
graded_decisions.comment: Simple broad-market exposure was the best-performing single holding this week for exactly the reason QQQ lagged -- it captured the rotation into cyclicals/broad market rather than fighting it. Only 3 shares (10.9% of account) -- position is too small to meaningfully offset the concentrated AI/tech risk elsewhere in the book.

patterns: CRITICAL -- the decision journal (journal/decisions.jsonl) has zero entries, ever, despite the account holding 4 live positions and at least one confirmed fill (QQQ, 2026-06-16). The daily routine is not writing decisions/reasoning to the log the README specifies, which means this review has no session-level reasoning to grade against and the 'read prior decisions at session start' feedback loop the whole system depends on is currently non-functional.

No trades occurred in the past 7 days (alpaca orders --hours 168 returned empty) and only 2 fills exist in the past 30 days (both 2026-06-16: QQQ buy, AAPL sell). The book has been static through a genuinely volatile 2.5-week stretch -- NVDA absorbed a fundamental negative catalyst (China GPU market ceded to Huawei) and MSFT round-tripped -17% then +10.6% -- with no trims, adds, or stop-outs in response.

The basket is concentrated in correlated AI/tech-adjacent exposure: MSFT + NVDA + QQQ together are ~65% of long market value ($6,231.72 of $8,466.06), and all three carry unrealized losses since entry while the lone broad-market position (SPY) is the only winner. The 'diversified basket' is effectively one leveraged bet on AI/tech sentiment plus a small market hedge.
suggested_adjustments: Fix the decision journal first: every session (including 'hold'/'skip' days) must append an entry to journal/decisions.jsonl per the README schema -- symbol, action, reasoning, research_cited. Until this happens, no future weekly review can actually grade decisions against stated theses, only against price action after the fact.

Treat NVDA's China-market-share loss and falling GPU lease pricing as a fundamental thesis break, not noise -- define an explicit invalidation/stop level for the position (currently -10.25% and the account's largest single-name exposure) rather than defaulting to hold.

Cap combined AI-hardware + AI-software + tech-heavy-ETF exposure (NVDA + MSFT + QQQ) at a target % of equity before adding new correlated longs -- right now that bucket is ~67% of equity and all three names sold off together in June for related reasons (AI capex/margin worries).

Don't treat a single sharp reversal week as thesis confirmation without corroboration: MSFT's +10.6% bounce is attributed by sources to sector rotation ahead of its late-July earnings print, not new fundamentals -- require 2+ outlets and/or the actual earnings print before upgrading the thesis from 'oversold bounce' to 'trend resumed.'
summary: No decisions were logged this week (journal/decisions.jsonl is empty) and no trades were placed (last fill 2026-06-16) -- the four open positions (MSFT, NVDA, QQQ, SPY) simply rode the week's price action. Portfolio +1.32% vs SPY +1.57%, VT +0.84%, SSO +2.94% (SSO's 2x-vs-SPY relationship held roughly: 2.94% vs. 2x1.57%=3.14%, a small daily-reset drag). NVDA's decline looks structurally driven (China share loss, margin compression) and is the account's biggest problem position; QQQ's weekly lag looks like short-term sector rotation rather than a trend break; SPY and MSFT's bounce were the bright spots. The single biggest issue this week isn't any individual trade -- it's that the decision log the whole review process depends on is empty, so there is nothing to actually grade against stated reasoning.

graded_decisions.news_since: Azure/AI thesis has not been fundamentally refuted (Azure +40% YoY held up across two subsequent earnings prints, AI ARR +123% YoY), but the stock round-tripped from $425 entry to a $460 peak (6/1) down to a $352 trough (6/25) on $190B AI-capex/FCF-compression fears, then partly recovered to $390 on a chips-to-software rotation ahead of late-July earnings.
graded_decisions.comment: PROCESS FAILURE, not a thesis failure: the 5/19 entry explicitly set 'stock closes below $400' as the invalidation level. MSFT closed below $400 on 2026-06-10 and kept falling to $352 -- that trigger was never revisited in any subsequent session. On 6/17 the routine set a NEW self-imposed trigger ('watch $380 for break-below trim'); MSFT closed at $365 on 6/24 and $352 on 6/25, decisively through that level too, and still no trim happened. Research quality each day was genuinely good (fresh, specific data, not boilerplate) -- the gap is between research and enforcement.

graded_decisions.news_since: Concentration was actively and correctly managed early (trimmed from ~45% to ~34% of equity across 5/28 and 6/2 on post-earnings/post-COMPUTEX strength). But from early June onward, Nvidia conceded China's advanced AI chip market to Huawei and B200 GPU lease pricing fell ~31% ($6.11->$4.22/hr, 5/30-6/21), signaling real margin compression -- despite another record ~$82B revenue quarter.
graded_decisions.comment: Good sizing discipline early, but no further de-risking once the thesis shifted from 'earnings momentum' to 'structural China/margin erosion' in June. Largest single-name position in the book; still the account's biggest loser by percentage terms.

graded_decisions.news_since: Entered on an Iran/Strait-of-Hormuz oil-supply-shock thesis (5/21). Six days later, the IEA's May report showed global oil demand contracting (-2.4 mb/d YoY in Q2) and a US-Iran deal reduced the war premium -- thesis invalidated almost immediately.
graded_decisions.comment: Good exit discipline (cut it fast rather than holding through hope) even though the entry itself was a quick loser. XLE kept falling after the exit too (to $53.24 by 7/2, another -6.7%), so the exit timing was correct. On 6/30 the routine considered re-entering XLE citing 'Brent ~$105/bbl' and 'XLE +38.2% YTD,' but actual XLE closes show a steady decline the entire period with no such spike -- worth checking why that price claim didn't match the ETF's real chart before it nearly triggered a re-entry.

graded_decisions.news_since: Built an 8-share position over two weeks entirely on anticipation of the June 8 WWDC AI/Siri reveal, citing Street-high analyst price targets ($400 Wedbush, $380 BofA) and 'agentic AI' upside. WWDC delivered a Siri overhaul that arrived roughly two years late and was blocked by EU and China regulation across ~35% of iPhone shipments -- a clear disappointment.
graded_decisions.comment: Textbook buy-the-rumor-sell-the-news: added into a well-telegraphed catalyst that was already reflected in raised price targets, then closed at a loss when the actual event undershot the hype baked into those targets.

graded_decisions.news_since: Nasdaq-100 underperformed the S&P 500 in the most recent week on a 'Great Rotation' out of semis/AI-hardware profit-taking into cyclicals, compounded by a weak June jobs print (+57K vs. consensus). Sources read this as short-term rotation, not a durable leadership change.
graded_decisions.comment: This is the second QQQ entry -- the first 1-share lot (bought 5/28 at ~$735.64) was sold 6/3 at ~$744.21 (a small +1.2% gain) specifically to fund more AAPL, which then lost 4.4% on the WWDC bust. Net effect of that rotation: gave up a working diversifier to add to a losing conviction bet. The current 3-share lot (bought 6/16 for the same 'diversify away from single-name AI risk' reason) is down 4.1% since entry, mostly from being bought right before this rotation-driven pullback.

graded_decisions.news_since: Broad market hit record highs this week on rotation into cyclicals and a weak jobs report favoring defensive positioning; no session-level research was ever logged for SPY specifically because it required none.
graded_decisions.comment: The one position with zero active management and zero research citations across 45 sessions is the single best performer in the book, and it beat every actively-managed name. Only 3 shares (~11% of equity) -- too small to have offset the losses elsewhere.

patterns: INVALIDATION LEVELS ARE SET BUT NEVER ENFORCED. MSFT's 5/19 entry set 'stock closes below $400' as its stop; MSFT closed below $400 on 6/10 and fell to $352 by 6/25 without a single session flagging the breach. A second, self-imposed trigger set on 6/17 ('$380 break-below = trim') was breached on 6/24-6/25 and also never acted on. This is the single most concrete, fixable failure in the whole history: research is genuinely updated with fresh data every session, but the stated triggers from prior sessions are never checked against current price before deciding to hold again.

Catalyst-anticipation trades lose money twice in the same way: AAPL was built up over two weeks purely ahead of WWDC on Street-high analyst targets, then closed at a loss when the event disappointed (-4.4%). XLE was bought on an Iran/Hormuz war-shock narrative and invalidated within 6 days by IEA demand data (-5.2%). Both are 'buy the well-telegraphed catalyst, thesis already priced in, event undershoots' failures.

Whipsaw churn between QQQ and AAPL: bought 1 QQQ (5/28), sold it 6 days later to fund more AAPL (6/3), then closed all of AAPL and bought QQQ again (6/16) -- three moves between the same two vehicles in three weeks, netting a modest loss overall once the AAPL leg is included.

Concentration risk was well-managed early (NVDA trimmed from ~45% to ~34% of equity across two sessions in late May) but that discipline was never reapplied after 6/2, even as new fundamental risks emerged on both MSFT (capex/FCF compression) and NVDA (China market exit) through June.

The single unmanaged, un-researched position (SPY, held flat for 45 sessions) outperformed every actively-managed name and the whole book. The portfolio's entire active management layer has subtracted value relative to just holding the passive core.
suggested_adjustments: Make invalidation checks mechanical, not narrative: every session must explicitly compare current price to any invalidation/trigger level set in a prior session for each open position, and if breached, the default action is trim/close -- not a fresh 'thesis still intact' paragraph. This alone would have caught both MSFT breaches.
If a position is down >15% from entry AND has breached its own stated trigger, require an actual size reduction before the position is allowed to just 'hold' again in the next session.

Discount well-telegraphed catalysts (WWDC, COMPUTEX, earnings) that are already reflected in raised analyst price targets going into the event -- require the outcome to beat the re-priced expectation, not just occur, before sizing into it. This would have blocked the AAPL WWDC add.

Stop rotating capital between overlapping vehicles (QQQ vs. direct mega-cap names) to fund a different conviction trade -- pick single-name or broad-ETF exposure per thesis and hold it, rather than swapping mid-stream.
summary: Full-history review (2026-05-19 to 2026-07-03, 45 sessions -- the first time this data has been visible on main, having been recovered from 44 previously-orphaned branches). Portfolio is down -6.80% since the $10,000 inception while every passive benchmark is positive: SPY +1.51%, VT +2.29%, SSO +2.33% (SSO also shows a real leverage-decay gap here -- 2.33% actual vs. ~3.01% theoretical 2xSPY, a meaningful drag from six-plus weeks of choppy, directionless price action). Alpha vs. SPY is -8.31 points. The research quality underlying each day's decisions is genuinely good and specific, not boilerplate -- the failure is structural: stated invalidation levels (MSFT's $400 stop, then its own $380 trim-trigger) are set and then never checked again, two separate catalyst-anticipation trades (AAPL/WWDC, XLE/Hormuz) lost money in the same 'priced-in event disappoints' pattern, and the single passive, unmanaged position (SPY) quietly beat every actively-managed name in the book.
